pedestrian_crossing_compliance_measureme.claim_text: Unsignalized midblock crosswalks are the most preferred pedestrian facility (83% reported preference) with high crossing compliance rate (71.2%)
Crosswalk location relative to pedestrian origin/destination is the most influential decision factor (90% said so)
Pedestrian signal presence significantly influences crossing decisions at specific locations (74% reported influence)
Physical barriers (vegetation and concrete walls) influence pedestrian crossing decisions significantly (65% reported influence)
Good agreement exists between self-reported survey behavior and actual observed pedestrian behavior

Pedestrian road-crossing behavior simula.claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with adjusted simulation results closely matching field observations (mean red light runner proportion: 0.82 simulated vs 0.85 field)

Pedestrians can be effectively classified into two types—law-obeying and opportunistic—with the proportion of opportunistic pedestrians varying based on external situational factors such as policeman presence, traffic volume, and other pedestrians' behavior

The violation follower phenomenon (where law-obeying pedestrians follow others to violate traffic signals) significantly affects overall red light running rates, with 69% of Type I pedestrians potentially becoming followers when witnessing violations

Questionnaire-based parameter calibration alone is insufficient, as self-reported behavior differs from actual behavior, requiring adjustment of the law-obeying proportion parameter from 0.55 to 0.35 to achieve better simulation-field data alignment

Pedestrian trajectory estimation.claim_text: The desired direction model using Weibull distribution enables realistic representation of pedestrian maneuver toward predetermined positions at crosswalk, incorporating crosswalk geometry, pedestrian origin-destination movement, and density factors.
The modified social force model achieves 5.7% MAPE for position estimation and 10.5% for speed estimation, demonstrating accurate reproduction of pedestrian trajectories at signalized crosswalk.

The model successfully reproduces lane-formation phenomenon with 3.5 lanes (vs 2.8 observed) using route-choice based desired direction, and represents collision avoidance behavior with left-turning vehicles.

The separation of parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups with respective calibration methods provides effective parameter estimation with all parameters significant at 95% confidence level.
The coefficient for vehicle repulsive force interaction strength has the largest parameter value, indicating highest sensitivity of pedestrian behavior to collision avoidance with vehicles.

pedestrian_road_crossing_behavior_simula.claim_text: The developed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running (45-83% violation rate) and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities

Classifying pedestrians into law-obeying and opportunistic types with dynamic proportions based on external factors (policeman presence, other pedestrians' behavior, vehicle flow) improves simulation realism
The time gap criterion (DT) for pedestrian crossing decisions can be effectively extracted from videotape data using the proposed calculation method

After parameter adjustment based on analyzing deviations between initial simulation results and field data, the simulation results agree well with field observations (mean red light runner proportion: 0.82 simulation vs 0.85 videotape)

Pedestrian crossing decision at crosswal.claim_text: Inclusion of external features significantly increased pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs
Walking silhouette and 'braking' in text were the most favored visual interfaces, while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who frequently commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAVs poorly even with external features
VR setting works well as a valuable mode for research involving FAVs and pedestrians

Pedestrian-FAV interaction evaluation.claim_text: Inclusion of external features significantly increased pedestrians' receptivity toward FAVs

Pedestrians who frequently commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAVs poorly even with external features

Predict pedestrians' violating road-cros.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention in all four models (p<0.001 in all cases)
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best model fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030, CMIN/DF=1.235)
Subjective norm was significant in Model 1 (basic TPB) but became non-significant when descriptive norm was added to Model 2, indicating descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention (p=0.004), indicating that pedestrians are influenced by other pedestrians' violating behavior
Perceived behavioral control and perceived risk were not significant predictors of violating crossing behavior intention in any model

Pedestrian crossing at signalized inters.claim_text: Text distraction causes the greatest impairment to pedestrian crossing performance, followed by phone conversation distraction and music distraction
All three mobile phone distractions significantly increase pedestrian crossing initiation time compared to undistracted condition
Music distraction increases pupil diameter and reduces scanning frequency and visual attention toward traffic signal area
Phone conversation distraction leads to slower crossing speed and reduced fixation points toward right traffic area and less fixation time/duration on left traffic area

Text distraction significantly impairs visual attention allocation, reducing fixation points, fixation time and fixation duration across all traffic-related areas (crosswalk, traffic signal, left and right traffic areas)

analyze_pedestrian_red_light_violation_d.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower red light running propensity compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649)
Presence of a companion reduces red light running propensity by approximately 22.8% (elasticity=-0.228), while increase in number of pedestrians around reduces it by 0.94% per 1% increase
Red light running propensity increases significantly when other pedestrians violate the red light in the same cycle (b=1.029, odds ratio=2.798), demonstrating strong social influence effect
Time to green and red time duration both positively correlate with increased red light running propensity (b=0.090 and b=0.060 respectively)
Higher traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles reduce red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively), indicating pedestrian sensitivity to traffic danger

modeling_pedestrian_waiting_times.claim_text: Pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections follow a U-shaped distribution, characterized by a large proportion crossing immediately and a large proportion waiting for the entire red-man phase
The proposed mixture distribution model with bounded Pareto components can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios

The model successfully differentiates between risk-taking and risk-averse pedestrians based on their effective critical headway and sensitivity to vehicle gaps, with practical implications for pedestrian safety
The commonly used exponential distribution and its extensions are inadequate for modeling bounded pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections
Empirical analysis shows that younger pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with significant differences in waiting behavior across age and gender groups

pedestrian_behavior_analysis.claim_text: Pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections follow a U-shaped distribution, characterized by a large proportion crossing immediately and a large proportion waiting for the entire red-man phase
The proposed mixture distribution model with bounded Pareto components can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios

The model successfully differentiates between risk-taking and risk-averse pedestrians based on their effective critical headway and sensitivity to vehicle gaps, with practical implications for pedestrian safety
The commonly used exponential distribution and its extensions are inadequate for modeling bounded pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections
Empirical analysis shows that younger pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with significant differences in waiting behavior across age and gender groups

identify_significant_factors_affecting_r.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period in a day (daytime vs dawn), attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior
Pedestrians in a hurry are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio 0.299), indicating time pressure is a major factor
Daytime has the highest red-light running probability compared to dawn, dusk, and night periods
Pedestrians who believe road facility quality affects their crossing behavior are more likely to run red lights (odds ratio 0.483)
Strolling pedestrians are safer (lower red-light running probability) compared to those going to work or school (odds ratio 0.394)

predict_probability_of_red_light_running.claim_text: Four variables significantly affect pedestrian red-light running probability: trip purpose (stroll vs work/school), time period in a day (daytime vs dawn), attitude towards running red light when in hurry, and attitude towards whether road facility quality affects crossing behavior

Pedestrian red light running violation a.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
Presence of other pedestrians violating red light in the same cycle significantly increases individual red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023 for Model 1; 2.797 for Model 2)
Older pedestrians have higher propensity of red light running compared to normal adults (odds ratio = 1.208), while adolescents have lower propensity (odds ratio = 0.834)
Higher traffic volume and higher percentage of heavy vehicles are associated with reduced red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively)
Significant interaction effects exist between gender and traffic volume, gender and other violators, elderly and companion, and other violators and companion on red light running propensity

Vehicle-pedestrian interaction modeling.claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, with maximum relative error less than 10% compared to empirical data
Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of traffic lanes and vehicular volume but decreases with pedestrian volume, while vehicular delay increases with all three factors
Signal control is recommended when vehicular volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h based on 20s vehicular delay threshold (LOS B)
The model converges probabilistic VY and PGA decision models into the car-following framework, enabling analysis of traffic flow impacts from vehicle-pedestrian interactions

Analyze pedestrian crossing speed variat.claim_text: Pedestrian crossing speed at signalized intersections varies significantly from the IRC standard of 1.2 m/s, with design crossing speeds of 0.95 m/s for old pedestrians and 1.12 m/s for adult pedestrians
Gender, age, and departure signal phase are statistically significant factors (p<0.05) affecting pedestrian crossing speed variations based on ANOVA test results
Pedestrian gender and group size are the most significant factors affecting pedestrian compliance behavior, with male pedestrians and single pedestrians showing higher noncompliance rates
Approaching vehicle type and suitable gap between pedestrian and vehicle are significant factors (p<0.05) affecting pedestrian-vehicular interactions in crosswalks

Pedestrian crossing decision recognition.claim_text: The DBN+PF system can recognize crossing decisions within 2 seconds of observation and waiting decisions within 3-4 seconds across all noise levels with >85% accuracy
The proposed method achieves >86% recognition accuracy for both cross and wait decisions under different measurement noise levels (rn = 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 m)
The DBN model reduces positioning errors from noisy measurements, achieving mean errors of 0.08m (rn=0.1m) and 0.47m (rn=1.0m) with standard deviations of 0.07m and 0.31m respectively
The model integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian behavior in a probabilistic framework, mimicking human decision-making processes
The system outperforms the baseline Iryo-Asano model after 2.0 seconds of observation time, particularly for recognizing waiting behaviors

Model pedestrian speed change events (ac.claim_text: The proposed model can represent pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec) and current speed, conflict area dummy, and PFG phase have significant impacts on acceleration/deceleration choices
The pedestrian speed change model provides significantly higher vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumptions when integrated with vehicle maneuver models
The PFG interval is critical for safety assessment as it determines high probability of pedestrian behavioral changes

claims.claim_text: The proposed model successfully represents the high rate of pedestrian red light running and mixed traffic characteristics in Chinese cities, with adjusted simulation results closely matching field observations (mean red light runner proportion: 0.82 simulated vs 0.85 field)

Pedestrians can be effectively classified into two types—law-obeying and opportunistic—with the proportion of opportunistic pedestrians varying based on external situational factors such as policeman presence, traffic volume, and other pedestrians' behavior

The violation follower phenomenon (where law-obeying pedestrians follow others to violate traffic signals) significantly affects overall red light running rates, with 69% of Type I pedestrians potentially becoming followers when witnessing violations

Questionnaire-based parameter calibration alone is insufficient, as self-reported behavior differs from actual behavior, requiring adjustment of the law-obeying proportion parameter from 0.55 to 0.35 to achieve better simulation-field data alignment

claims.claim_text: The desired direction model using Weibull distribution enables realistic representation of pedestrian maneuver toward predetermined positions at crosswalk, incorporating crosswalk geometry, pedestrian origin-destination movement, and density factors.
The modified social force model achieves 5.7% MAPE for position estimation and 10.5% for speed estimation, demonstrating accurate reproduction of pedestrian trajectories at signalized crosswalk.

The model successfully reproduces lane-formation phenomenon with 3.5 lanes (vs 2.8 observed) using route-choice based desired direction, and represents collision avoidance behavior with left-turning vehicles.

The separation of parameters into measurable and non-measurable groups with respective calibration methods provides effective parameter estimation with all parameters significant at 95% confidence level.
The coefficient for vehicle repulsive force interaction strength has the largest parameter value, indicating highest sensitivity of pedestrian behavior to collision avoidance with vehicles.

claims.claim_text: Instrumental attitude was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention in all four models (p<0.001 in all cases)
Model 4 (containing instrumental attitude, descriptive norm, and conformity tendency) provided the best model fit (CFI=0.996, RMSEA=0.030, CMIN/DF=1.235)
Subjective norm was significant in Model 1 (basic TPB) but became non-significant when descriptive norm was added to Model 2, indicating descriptive norm is a stronger predictor than subjective norm
Conformity tendency was a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior intention (p=0.004), indicating that pedestrians are influenced by other pedestrians' violating behavior
Perceived behavioral control and perceived risk were not significant predictors of violating crossing behavior intention in any model

claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower red light running propensity compared to males (b=-0.433, odds ratio=0.649)
Presence of a companion reduces red light running propensity by approximately 22.8% (elasticity=-0.228), while increase in number of pedestrians around reduces it by 0.94% per 1% increase
Red light running propensity increases significantly when other pedestrians violate the red light in the same cycle (b=1.029, odds ratio=2.798), demonstrating strong social influence effect
Time to green and red time duration both positively correlate with increased red light running propensity (b=0.090 and b=0.060 respectively)
Higher traffic volume and percentage of heavy vehicles reduce red light running propensity (elasticities of -0.518 and -0.484 respectively), indicating pedestrian sensitivity to traffic danger

claims.claim_text: Pedestrians' intended waiting times at signalized intersections follow a U-shaped distribution, characterized by a large proportion crossing immediately and a large proportion waiting for the entire red-man phase
The proposed mixture distribution model with bounded Pareto components can reduce to J-shaped or L-shaped distributions under certain traffic scenarios

The model successfully differentiates between risk-taking and risk-averse pedestrians based on their effective critical headway and sensitivity to vehicle gaps, with practical implications for pedestrian safety
The commonly used exponential distribution and its extensions are inadequate for modeling bounded pedestrian waiting times at signalized intersections
Empirical analysis shows that younger pedestrians tend to be risk-takers while elderly pedestrians are more risk-averse, with significant differences in waiting behavior across age and gender groups

claims.claim_text: Female pedestrians have significantly lower propensity of red light running compared to males (odds ratio = 0.667)
Presence of other pedestrians violating red light in the same cycle significantly increases individual red light running propensity (odds ratio = 4.023 for Model 1; 2.797 for Model 2)

claims.claim_text: The proposed model accurately describes the interaction between vehicle yielding and pedestrian gap acceptance behaviors at unsignalized midblock crosswalks, with maximum relative error less than 10% compared to empirical data
Pedestrian waiting time increases with the number of traffic lanes and vehicular volume but decreases with pedestrian volume, while vehicular delay increases with all three factors
Signal control is recommended when vehicular volume exceeds 480 veh/h/ln or pedestrian volume exceeds 500 ped/h based on 20s vehicular delay threshold (LOS B)
The model converges probabilistic VY and PGA decision models into the car-following framework, enabling analysis of traffic flow impacts from vehicle-pedestrian interactions

contradictions.task: Pedestrian crossing decision recognition (cross vs wait)
contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: The DBN+PF system can recognize crossing decisions within 2 seconds of observation and waiting decisions within 3-4 seconds across all noise levels with >85% accuracy
The proposed method achieves >86% recognition accuracy for both cross and wait decisions under different measurement noise levels (rn = 0.1, 0.4, 1.0 m)
The DBN model reduces positioning errors from noisy measurements, achieving mean errors of 0.08m (rn=0.1m) and 0.47m (rn=1.0m) with standard deviations of 0.07m and 0.31m respectively
The model integrates contextual information (traffic signal phases) with pedestrian behavior in a probabilistic framework, mimicking human decision-making processes
The system outperforms the baseline Iryo-Asano model after 2.0 seconds of observation time, particularly for recognizing waiting behaviors

contradictions.reason: Multiple claim types detected under the same task; manual review recommended.

claims.claim_text: The proposed model can represent pedestrian travel time distribution more accurately than the constant speed model
The difference between necessary speed to complete crossing (Vnec) and current speed, conflict area dummy, and PFG phase have significant impacts on acceleration/deceleration choices
The pedestrian speed change model provides significantly higher vehicle yield rates compared to constant speed assumptions when integrated with vehicle maneuver models
The PFG interval is critical for safety assessment as it determines high probability of pedestrian behavioral changes
